fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Traditional actor critic RL algorithms, require training both an actor and a critic (as the name implies). Typically, these components are both of equal size. In the field of RL, this is non-problematic because models are typically rather small (at least in comparison to LLMs). In DeepSeekMath: Pushing the limits of mathematical reasoning in open language models

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: \begin {align*} {\mathcal {J}}_{\mathrm {GRPO}}(\theta )&= \mathbb {E}[q\sim P(Q),\{o_{i}\}_{i=1}^{G}\sim \pi _{\theta _{o l d}}(O|q)] \\ &= \frac {1}{G}\sum _{i=1}^{G}\left (\operatorname *{min}\left (\frac {\pi _{\theta }(o_{i}|q)}{\pi _{\theta _{o d}}(o_{i}|q)}A_{i},\operatorname *{clip}\left (\frac {\pi _{\theta }(o_{i}|q)}{\pi _{\theta _{o d d}}(o_{i}|q)},1-\varepsilon ,1+\varepsilon \right )A_{i}\right )-\beta \mathbb {D}_{K L}\left (\pi _{\theta }||\pi _{r e f}\right )\right ) \end {align*} where \mathbb {D}_{\mathrm {K L}}\left (\pi _{\theta }||\pi _{\mathrm {ref}}\right )=\frac {\pi _{\mathrm {ref}}(o_{i}|q)}{\pi _{\theta }(o_{i}|q)}-\log \frac {\pi _{\mathrm {ref}}(o_{i}|q)}{\pi _{\theta }(o_{i}|q)}-1 The astute RL reader will notice this is essentially PPO. The key distinction here is that the advantage A_i is not computed using a critic model. Instead, A_{i}=\frac {r_{i}-\mathrm {mean}(\{r_{1},r_{2},\cdots ,r_{G}\})}{\mathrm {std}(\{r_{1},r_{2},\cdots ,r_{G}\})}.

fr:title.text: DeepSeekMath: Pushing the limits of mathematical reasoning in open language models

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: DeepSeekMath: Pushing the limits of mathematical reasoning in open language models
fr:frontmatter.fr:meta: @misc{shao2024deepseekmathpushinglimitsmathematical, title = {DeepSeekMath: Pushing the Limits of Mathematical Reasoning in Open Language Models}, author = {Zhihong Shao and Peiyi Wang and Qihao Zhu and Runxin Xu and Junxiao Song and Xiao Bi and Haowei Zhang and Mingchuan Zhang and Y. K. Li and Y. Wu and Daya Guo}, year = {2024}, url = {https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.03300}, primaryclass = {cs.CL}, archiveprefix = {arXiv}, eprint = {2402.03300} }

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Proximal policy optimization algorithms

fr:title.text: Deepseek v1 through R1: RL is back!

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Deepseek v1 through R1: RL is back!

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: In this blog, we will aim to understand the key contributions of DeepSeek-R1: Incentivizing reasoning capability in LLMs via reinforcement learning. It will serve as the complement to my group meeting presentation possibly consisting of more in-depth explanations. Time permitting, we might go over the engineering innovations introduced in DeepSeek-V3 technical report.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: TODO. Kinda wanna look into the architectural / training innovations from this paper.

fr:title.text: How is R1 different then previous iterations of models?

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: How is R1 different then previous iterations of models?

fr:ul.fr:li: In R1-Zero, they do ZERO SFT on the base model - directly apply reinforcement learning.
Use PPO like policy optimization but do NOT learn a reward model.

Use very simple reward:
+1 for correct answer
-0.5 for incorrect answer
-1 for inability to answer.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Traditional actor critic RL algorithms, require training both an actor and a critic (as the name implies). Typically, these components are both of equal size. In the field of RL, this is non-problematic because models are typically rather small (at least in comparison to LLMs). In DeepSeekMath: Pushing the limits of mathematical reasoning in open language models

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: \begin {align*} {\mathcal {J}}_{\mathrm {GRPO}}(\theta )&= \mathbb {E}[q\sim P(Q),\{o_{i}\}_{i=1}^{G}\sim \pi _{\theta _{o l d}}(O|q)] \\ &= \frac {1}{G}\sum _{i=1}^{G}\left (\operatorname *{min}\left (\frac {\pi _{\theta }(o_{i}|q)}{\pi _{\theta _{o d}}(o_{i}|q)}A_{i},\operatorname *{clip}\left (\frac {\pi _{\theta }(o_{i}|q)}{\pi _{\theta _{o d d}}(o_{i}|q)},1-\varepsilon ,1+\varepsilon \right )A_{i}\right )-\beta \mathbb {D}_{K L}\left (\pi _{\theta }||\pi _{r e f}\right )\right ) \end {align*} where \mathbb {D}_{\mathrm {K L}}\left (\pi _{\theta }||\pi _{\mathrm {ref}}\right )=\frac {\pi _{\mathrm {ref}}(o_{i}|q)}{\pi _{\theta }(o_{i}|q)}-\log \frac {\pi _{\mathrm {ref}}(o_{i}|q)}{\pi _{\theta }(o_{i}|q)}-1 The astute RL reader will notice this is essentially PPO. The key distinction here is that the advantage A_i is not computed using a critic model. Instead, A_{i}=\frac {r_{i}-\mathrm {mean}(\{r_{1},r_{2},\cdots ,r_{G}\})}{\mathrm {std}(\{r_{1},r_{2},\cdots ,r_{G}\})}.

fr:ul.fr:li: Reinforcement Learning for all Scenarios: Seems like they do RLHF after the pure RL stage.
Do traditional helpfulness harmfulness RLHF with trained reward model.
To distill, they do only SFT with R1 generated COT.
They show that distillation outperforms doing pure RL approach on smaller model
Seems contradictory to 7B model and 8K examples: Emerging reasoning with reinforcement learning is both effective and efficient

fr:title.text: The History and Evolution of Policy Gradient Algorithms

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The History and Evolution of Policy Gradient Algorithms

fr:mainmatter.fr:p:
Rough itinerary,
Vanilla policy gradient
Policy gradient theorem + proof
Deterministic policy gradient theorem + (maybe)proof
Actor critic method
A2C: Variance reduction method
(Maybe) A3C: Asynchronous update
Trust region policy optimization
Soft Actor Critic
Proximal Policy Optimization
Group Relative Policy Optimization
